nativeRange.habitat: Riparian and marshland environments along streams, ponds, and rice paddies — grows emergent at the water's edge but tolerates fully submerged conditions

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich substrate preferred but tolerates plain gravel or sand
best in dense groups of multiple stems for a fuller display

avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as goldfish, silver dollars, or large herbivorous cichlids

summary: Hygrophila siamensis is a fast-growing, narrow-leaved stem plant from Thailand and Southeast Asia, widely treated by horticultural sources as a variety of Hygrophila corymbosa. An easy, undemanding background or tall midground plant — tolerant of low to high light and not requiring CO2, but responsive to both.
careNotes: Hygrophila siamensis is sold as a distinct species in the aquarium trade but is treated by Wikipedia and Aquasabi as a variety/ecological form of Hygrophila corymbosa — distinguished primarily by its narrow, lanceolate submerged leaves rather than the broader leaves of the standard corymbosa form. Care is essentially identical to H. corymbosa: it is an undemanding beginner stem plant tolerant of a wide temperature window (Aquasabi cites 18-30°C; the 22-28°C window above reflects the typical recommended aquarium range) and pH from roughly 5 to 7.5, with broad hardness tolerance. Light demand is flexible — Buceplant lists moderate to high, Aquasabi medium with tolerance for lower light — and CO2 is not required but accelerates growth and produces fuller stems. Substrate is flexible (plain gravel through nutrient-rich aquasoil), but balanced macro/micronutrient dosing — particularly iron — keeps leaves from yellowing under brighter lighting. Growth is fast and frequent trimming is needed to control height and to encourage lateral branching for a denser stand. Best displayed in dense groups in the background or tall midground. Minimum tank volume of 75 L is an estimated guideline reflecting the plant's adult height; no source specified an exact figure. Note that some nurseries also market this form under the trade name 'Stricta', which adds to the long-standing taxonomic ambiguity surrounding the corymbosa species complex.
breedingNotes: Propagation is straightforward via stem cuttings: trim a healthy top or lateral shoot 10-15 cm long, remove the lowest pair of leaves, and replant directly into the substrate. New roots form quickly. Regular topping and replanting of cuttings maintains a dense stand and rejuvenates older stems whose bases have lost leaves.

primary.name: Aquasabi
primary.notes: Primary source. Lists the plant as Hygrophila corymbosa 'Siamensis' — explicitly one of many varieties of H. corymbosa, also marketed as H. corymbosa 'Stricta', distinguished by lanceolate submerged leaves. Provides temperature 18-30°C, pH 5-7, gH 0-30°dGH, kH 2-18°dKH, recommended height 15-60 cm, medium light, fast growth, propagation via cuttings, background/midground placement.

additional.name: Buceplant
additional.notes: Listed as Hygrophila siamensis (own species name). Provides care level easy, pH 6-7.5, height 6-12 inches (~15-30 cm in trim), moderate-to-high light, CO2 not required, fast growth, propagation by stem cuttings, family Acanthaceae, Southeast Asia origin. Did not flag synonymy with corymbosa.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Acanthaceae, Lamiales), native range (Malaysia, Thailand, Singapore, Indonesia, India), riparian/emergent habitat, and the synonymy statement: 'There are several forms in the aquarium trade such as red, siamensis compacta and many others, that seem to be mainly ecological variants' — treating siamensis as a trade form/ecological variant of H. corymbosa, not a separate species.
